item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-written, but when specific background is necessary to understand why the reader should care about an article, and then the background is elided, it's an exercise in futility. Downvote.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image belongs to HiMY SYeD, and is licensed under All Rights Reserved. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. +1 but wouldn't it be funny if it had the "city" tag twice? Haha no

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldn't "Cross City City Council" be mangled into "Cross City City City City Council Council"? Or is it being referred to in the pre-mangled document as "Cross City Council", which just sounds weird to me but might be a regional thing I guess?

Or maybe even the anomalous bullshit realizes "Cross City City City City Council Council" is just too much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta at least upvote this for the attention to detail and persistence with the writing of the document. I also like that the city council members all seem to have various names from different ethnic backgrounds, which adds to the study of other words from other languages and cultures coming into effect. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a fan of format screws where the format screw IS the skip. Other format screws go in interesting directions - underneath all the Allison Eckharts Allison Eckhart is still an interesting anomaly, and 2521 is still an interesting monster with a bunch of fridge horror thrown in as well. The anomaly in this is just "every C word repeats itself" which is just dull.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: containment breach

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All electronic documents which contain contain the exact text of any words spoken during meetings in said location duplicate all English words written inside of it that begin with the letter “c c,” including within acronyms

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the point…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's SCP not SCCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This scip makes me sick to my stomach to read. Like I'm trying to get over a bout of nausea now because every time I read this article I get nauseous. Good job though

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I'm very sorry about that, I was posting this on mobile, I edited it and it didn't take.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here goes.
The translation is a success if you ask me. The liberties taken are well-picked and well-placed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You may want to edit the post to include the link properly, since the link currently leads to the Example Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I translated this into Russian, just for the challenge, as the article is written to work specifically in English. I had to take a set of creative liberties and explain what it does with languages that didn't descend from Phoenician at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Silly effects" has never been the standard used for -J articles, though.

This is true. However, there is the occasional perception seen in new users that stuff on the mainlist needs to be super-serious, and anything remotely funny goes into the -J section.

All I was doing is explaining the thought process, might not have been clear on that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Silly effects" has never been the standard used for -J articles, though. If that were true, a good chunk of the mainlist would be deported over there. Considering that this is clearly meant as an in-universe phenomenon and that, again, there isn't really a joke here, I'm not seeing how this is somehow less suitable in its current designation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the issue is that it's obvious effect can be perceived as more silly than serious, and naturally lends itself to a similar kind of wordplay abuse as SCP-SAFE-J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really understand this statement. What about it makes you think it would work as a joke article? There isn't a joke here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would work more as a -J than a mainpage article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried not +1'ing, but it was humorous enough that I couldn't couldn't help myself
Congratulations congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was totally willing to do that, but wikidot is no fun and doesn't allow two of the same tag to exist on a page. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Would it be possible to tag this with city city twice?
Also, would the word "crosses" meaning more than one cross, not be duplicated, as opposed to "crosses crosses" which refers to an intersection or meeting?
Like, "East Road crosses crosses Pine Drive" and "Several crosses are in the church."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What constitutes an English word?

Can I mash my face on a keyboard and get the resulting jumble duplicated as long as it begins with C? Does it extend to neologisms and ad hoc words like "curiouser"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think if the Bible verse about Babel had been included in the article, it would have made the article much better and I probably would have upvoted. But as it stands right now, it's not very interesting. It's just a thing that duplicates words starting with 'c' except for a few church-y words and… that's it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really understand the story behind this, if there is one, but SCiPs that mess with articles are one of my favorites to read. And that note hurt my head, but I enjoyed the read.

+1

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
…Huh. I could've sworn I checked before I reverted that.

Good catch, undergroundmonorail.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
It shouldn't have been. Thanks for the heads up.

Edit: it's been rereverted. ;)

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
(I got permission from Eskobar in chat to reply to this staff post.)

I believe that you misunderstood the edit. The edit comment is "fix duplicate containment" which appears to mean "removed duplicate word". Actually, there was an instance of the word "containment" that was not duplicated, and the edit duplicated it. McMonkeyMcBean didn't miss the point of the skip, they fixed a legitimate error. The comment was misleading but I don't think it should have been reverted.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit was reverted, as it wasn't necessary. Any reviewers, please read the article closely before making any spelling changes. There are quite a few duplicated words; it's intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While it is not terribly complicated complicated, I find this containment containment rather charming charming charming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Will deliberate misspellings (Sity kouncil) or using the cyrillic "с" instead of "c" work?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite like this. It really doesn't need any ties beyond what's provided. Something in the local network is causing an odd duplication. My only question is: are files that get routed through internet traffic effected, and is this going to spread as a result?

Also, this completely trips into one of my favorite MPFC sketches:

Tourist: Yes I saw your advert in the bolour supplement.
Bounder: The what?
Tourist: The bolour supplement.
Bounder: The colour supplement?
Tourist: Yes I'm sorry I can't say the letter 'B'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the criticism that this article doesn't stand on its own is hard to argue with, but for me the implications being made with the list of unaffected words pushes it into upvote territory. I don't know what is going on, but as another user has said, some person or group somewhere is obviously trying something, and I love the fact that it could be anything from sinister to benign. I like the mystery of it, but I hope we one day get some more answers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whenever I see some kind of a format screw, I think 'alright, now to earn it.'. Like others have said, this doesn't do much beyond a format screw, and it doesn't make much sense without knowledge of the Dixieland world. I respect the originality and the attempt at worldbuilding, but I just didn't enjoy it.

That said, I enjoy how prolific of a writer you've been and I look forward to reading more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can Can someone explain me why this object class class is safe and not euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun. And that is always enough for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, even anomalous cults cults aren't perfect. Not the ever-escalating thrill ride I'd expect from DNM, but weird and non-threatening is great too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if it matters, but there's only one "collapse" in the collapsible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this question. class class-C C.

This:

beginning with the letter "c c."

shows that punctuation isn't duplicated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Additional anomalous effect: Turns users into Abed Nadir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So would class-C become class class-C-C, class-C class-C, or something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool Cool. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would've hated it if it weren't for the semi-format screw. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quite enjoyed this one! I like that this town is just kind of stuck with this bizarre language nuisance and are just making do for now.

This fragment: "beginning with the letter "c c." made me laugh at just how annoying this must be.

A shame you can't double the word "click" in "click to scroll to footnotes" as well. (Maybe you can, don't know.)

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't "corporation" be duplicated in the beginning of containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nothing but a gimmick

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having read the comments, I know you don't mean this to be part directly of the Dixieland Nightmare Magic series, but it still kind of doesn't stand on its own. It feels like an extranormal event or anomalous object given undue attention and focus, and pretty clearly ties into the Hyacinth Order thing to the degree that I would've downvoted it if I hadn't thought it tied into something bigger.

Now, having confirmed that it serves no larger worldbuilding purpose, my downvote comes because it just doesn't seem to be anything, despite being very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I nearly hit the edit button before I even got through the first sentence. It's like fishing for overeager pedants.

Also, how has nobody pointed out the FTML yet? Because that's brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love how my reaction to this article went from "have you even read an SCP before" to "oh my god I love this". There's something sublime to it, I'm not sure exactly why I like it so much, but I do. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I guess the researchers here felt differently. Remember, the effect of this infohazard manifests after an hour unobserved. Writing it with the document attached and saving a copy with the offending passages redacted out is a trivial measure and would leave you with this original.

¯\_(ツ)_/¯ I think we just differ on our views of the workflow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't think of a single reason in-universe for this article to have been written in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is an easily accessible (1st footnote) redacted article that would be included in most databases and compilations. This is the unredacted version of the article, presented out-of-universe because it's the more interesting one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would they keep the anomalous document in the SCP description? All that does is make the article gimmicky and hard to read (I understand that from an author's point of view, moving the document to a separate page would be boring (instead of stupid)).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually killed that one a minute ago! But thank you for pointing it out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
met in the Council Council Chambers of City City Hall

Shouldn't it be "Chambers Chambers?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The following Council Members were present

That should be "Council Council," methinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who hasn't read Dixieland Nightmare Magic, I actually really like this. It's a wonderful example of a restricted piece of work - there's no grand narrative here and nothing terribly interesting going on, at least on the surface. But it's a lovely bit of 'fluff', a tidbit of information showing how the way in which the Foundation works, day-to-day. Not everything has to be an ancient god stuck in a nuclear reactor or a spork haunted by the ghost of an obsessive hoarder. Sometimes the odd stuff just happens to be odd. There's actually quite a lot here, but it's not in the main body of the idea - the containment procedures, the infohazard warning for a format screw (the format screw itself I couldn't care less about, and largely ignored), the quirks of the effect which suggest, rather than explain, a mysterious background story.

I like this: I think it's neatly evocative of the absurdity of bureacrats (all these people are being forced to make their ridiculous legalese even more ridiculous, and no-one apparently minds), with a quaintly religious aspect that doesn't make any pretences at taking over the article. I like to think that whatever is behind the word-duplication has a deep-seated respect for Christianity, so it won't touch words related to it, and I like that as an idea.
